classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act incorporating the Town of Hustonville."
classification.summary: This act amends the incorporation of the Town of Hustonville by specifying that the town's affairs will be managed by five trustees. These trustees are to be elected annually by free white male inhabitants over the age of twenty-one who have resided in the town for sixty days. The act outlines the qualifications for trustees, their oath of office, how vacancies will be filled, and their corporate powers, including the ability to contract, sue, and be sued. It also grants them authority over town streets, alleys, sidewalks, and the power to levy and collect taxes. The act does not contain any provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race, beyond the mention of 'free white male inhabitants' in the election criteria.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that trustees are to be elected by 'free white male inhabitants'. While this is a racial qualifier, the primary purpose of the statute is to establish the governance of a town and its municipal affairs. It does not establish separate facilities, differential penalties, or restrictions on rights that are characteristic of Jim Crow laws. The racial qualifier is limited to the electorate for local trustees. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law, but the racial indicator is 'explicit' due to the mention of 'white male inhabitants'.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Stanford, in Lincoln County
classification.summary: This statute outlines the powers and duties of a police judge in the town of Stanford, Lincoln County. It details their oath of office, jurisdiction (within the town and 1.5 miles around it), powers in civil and criminal cases (similar to justices of the peace, but with specific provisions for criminal inquiry), jurisdiction over town by-laws, power to grant injunctions and other writs, duty to keep records, power to summon witnesses and issue compulsory process, power to fine and imprison for contempt (with limits), power to order juries, role as a conservator of the peace, power to take depositions, and a schedule of fees. The statute does not contain any provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the administrative and judicial functions of a police judge within a specific town and its surrounding area. It covers procedural aspects, jurisdiction, and fees. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that suggest differential treatment or segregation based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of [Town Name]
classification.summary: This statute outlines the duties, powers, and fees of a town marshal and police judge within a specific town. It details the marshal's responsibilities in serving process, collecting taxes and executions, and accounting for funds, with penalties for failure to perform. It also specifies the police judge's fees and the right of appeal from their judgments. Fines for ordinance violations are to be paid to the town treasurer.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details the administrative and judicial functions of a town government, specifically focusing on the roles and responsibilities of a town marshal and a police judge. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The statute appears to be a general municipal law concerning law enforcement and judicial procedures within a town. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate Covenant Lodge, No. 22.
classification.summary: This act incorporates Joseph D. Trapp, James Chrystal, J.B. Cooper, B. P. Watkins, James Dunlap, W. H. Newberry, A. W. Trebein, and Thomas A. Hornsey, and their associates, as a body corporate under the name of Covenant Lodge, No. 22, of the Independent Order of Odd Fellows. It grants them perpetual succession and the legal capacity to sue and be sued, contract, and have a common seal. They are also empowered to make and ordain regulations and by-laws for their government, provided they do not contravene the constitution and laws of the Grand Lodge of the Independent Order of Odd Fellows, or the constitution and laws of the United States or Kentucky.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to incorporate Elizabethtown," approved 9th February, 1850, and the several acts amendatory thereto.
classification.summary: This act amends the incorporation of Elizabethtown by modifying the powers of its trustees. It grants trustees the authority to remove the town marshal for neglect of duty, incapacity, or failure to provide bond, and to appoint a successor. It also specifies how a vacancy in the office of the police judge is to be filled, outlining the process of appointment by trustees and commission by the Governor. The act further details the fees for the police judge and marshal, aligning them with those of justices of the peace and constables, respectively. Finally, it empowers the trustees to levy and collect taxes on real estate and a poll tax on eligible male residents for the town's use.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute is an amendment to the act incorporating Elizabethtown. It details administrative and financial provisions for the town, such as the appointment and removal of town officials (marshal and police judge), their fees, and the town's taxing authority. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact the Charter of the Town of Highlands, in Campbell County
classification.summary: This statute outlines the powers and responsibilities of the trustees of the district of Highlands in Campbell County. It grants them authority to enact regulations for the good government and police of the district, including preventing stock from running at large, levying taxes for district purposes, managing and repairing public roads, and grading and paving roads. It also specifies how suits and actions related to the corporation should be prosecuted and defended, and how the boundaries of the district should be surveyed and marked. The statute does not contain any provisions related to race or racial segregation.

It focuses on governance, taxation, road maintenance, and legal proceedings for the district. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporatethe Town of Whitesville, in DaviessCounty
classification.summary: This act incorporates the town of Whitesville in Daviess County, Kentucky. It outlines the process for surveying the town's boundaries, electing trustees, and establishing the powers and duties of the Board of Trustees. The act specifies that qualified voters residing in the town will elect five trustees annually on the first Saturday in May. These trustees will form a body politic and corporate known as the 'Board of Trustees of the Town of Whitesville' and will have the power to contract, sue, and be sued. The chairman of the board will preside over meetings, and regular meetings are scheduled for the second Saturday of each month.

The confidence is high because the content is clearly about municipal incorporation and governance, with no racial implications.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for a railroad company to acquire land and materials for its construction through eminent domain. It details the procedures for summoning a jury to assess damages, the administration of oaths, the recording of verdicts, and the payment of compensation to landowners. It also addresses the company's responsibility to ensure that the construction of its road does not impede the passage of persons or property along any intersected established road or way, and to provide wagon ways across the land it passes through.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the legal process for eminent domain, specifically for a railroad company to acquire land. It details the procedures for jury selection, valuation of property, and compensation. There is no mention of race, and the procedures described are standard legal processes for land acquisition by corporations and do not appear to have any discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

The act also includes provisions regarding parallel lines and connections with other railroads.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Floyd's Fork Bridge Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Floyd's Fork Bridge Company, granting it the power to acquire land for road construction, assess damages, and appoint officers. It also allows the county court of Oldham County to subscribe to the company's capital stock for the purpose of building a bridge across Floyd's Fork.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Louisville and Bardstown Turnpike Road Company
classification.summary: This statute incorporates the Louisville and Bardstown Turnpike Road Company, granting it powers to hold capital stock, contract, sue and be sued, use a common seal, and perform acts necessary for its corporate object. It also details the authority of the president and directors to fix road elevation and grade, designate toll-gate locations, set and regulate toll rates (not exceeding general law), enter upon lands for examination and material acquisition, obtain rights-of-way, and initiate ad quod damnum proceedings to assess damages for land acquisition. Finally, it outlines the process for calling on stock payments.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the incorporation and operational powers of a turnpike road company. It details the company's legal standing, its ability to acquire land and materials, and the process for setting tolls and compensating landowners. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of the estates of John R. and William R. Woodfill
classification.summary: This act grants the administrator of the estates of John R. and William R. Woodfill, both deceased, the power to compound or compromise debts owed to the estates by doubtful or insolvent debtors. The administrator must file an affidavit with his settlements, along with at least one other person's affidavit, attesting that the compounded amount is the most that could be reasonably expected and that the debtor was insolvent or of doubtful solvency.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the Charter of the Shelby Railroad Company, and to authorize certain Districts in Shelby County to subscribe Stock in the same.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Shelby Railroad Company, granting its directors powers to contract with the Louisville and Frankfort Railroad Company for track usage. It also authorizes the Shelby County Court to subscribe stock in the Shelby Railroad Company, not exceeding $300,000, upon the request of the railroad company's directors and a favorable vote from qualified voters within specific districts of Shelby County. The act details the boundaries of these districts.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the amendment of a railroad company's charter and the authorization for a county to invest in it. It details contractual powers between railroad companies and the process for local government to subscribe stock, contingent on voter approval within defined districts. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Laws in Relation to Railroad Precincts
classification.summary: This act establishes two railroad precincts within a county and outlines the process for a county court to subscribe to railroad stock on behalf of taxpayers within these precincts. It details the voting procedures, tax levies, and assessment methods for these precincts. The act also specifies how poll books should be kept and votes counted, particularly for voters residing in election precincts that are divided by the railroad precinct lines.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the creation of two railroad precincts and the process for a county court to issue bonds for railroad stock, funded by local taxes. It details voting procedures and tax assessments. There is no mention of race, and the provisions apply to all qualified voters and property owners within the defined precincts. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1867.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled 'An Act to incorporate the Shelby Railroad Company,' approved March 10, 1860, and to authorize said company to issue bonds and to levy and collect taxes to pay the same.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Shelby Railroad Company to issue bonds and levy taxes to pay for them. It details the process for a subscription vote, the payment of subscriptions through bonds and taxes, and the collection of these taxes by the sheriff. Notably, Section 7 establishes a differential tax rate between railroad precinct number one and railroad precinct number two, with precinct number two being taxed at half the rate of precinct number one for every mill of ad valorem tax assessed.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financial operations of a railroad company, specifically the issuance of bonds and the levying of taxes to support it. While Section 7 introduces a differential tax rate between two precincts, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race or racial discrimination within the provided text. The differential taxation is based on geographic precincts, not on racial lines. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company
classification.summary: This act details provisions for the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company, including the issuance of stock certificates based on paid receipts, the imposition of taxes by county courts to fund bonds for the railroad, the role of the railroad treasurer as a receiver, the establishment of a sinking fund commission, collector compensation, tax assessment for lands within railroad precincts, and a condition regarding the collection of voluntary subscriptions made in 1859 and 1860. It specifies that voluntary subscriptions will not be collected from those taxed to pay for Shelby County's subscription, and this also applies to deceased subscribers whose estates are taxed.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses financial and administrative matters related to the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company, including taxation, stock issuance, and bond management. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1867.

The bonds are to be issued in sums between one hundred and one thousand dollars, bearing six percent annual interest, and payable within five to ten years. The court is required to levy an annual tax to cover interest and redeem the bonds, with any surplus invested to accumulate a fund for principal repayment. Issuance of bonds requires the concurrence of a majority of the county court justices.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of James and Thomas Forbush, of Henry County.
classification.summary: This act allows James and Thomas Forbush to inherit the estate of Stephen Bristow, a free man of color. Stephen Bristow's will devised his estate to his daughters, Betsey and Basha, who were slaves at the time of his death and therefore legally incapable of inheriting. Since all of Stephen's kindred were slaves, his estate escheated to the Commonwealth. Betsey died, leaving James and Thomas Forbush as her surviving children. This act enables James and Thomas Forbush to take and hold the estate as if they had been the legal heirs at the time of Stephen's death.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'free man of color' and 'slaves' in relation to inheritance. However, the core of the act is to rectify a situation where individuals were unable to inherit due to their enslaved status, and to allow their descendants to inherit. It does not establish or enforce discriminatory practices characteristic of Jim Crow laws. Instead, it appears to be a specific legislative remedy for a particular inheritance case that arose from the historical context of slavery. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'Act' passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the text clearly indicates the nature of the act and its lack of Jim Crow characteristics.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of the town of Columbus
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the town of Columbus by redefining the powers and duties of the mayor, including presiding over city council meetings, acting as a justice of the peace, and having the powers of a police judge. It also repeals provisions for the election of a register, auditor, and police judge, transferring their duties to the city council clerk and the mayor, respectively. The act also changes the election date for city officers and establishes a process for electing a temporary mayor in the mayor's absence.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the administrative and electoral structure of the town of Columbus. It details the roles and powers of the mayor and city council, and changes election procedures. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate the Richmond and Lexington Railroad Company.
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Richmond and Lexington Railroad Company, granting it perpetual succession and the powers necessary to construct and maintain a railroad. It outlines the capital stock, the process for electing a president and directors, and the appointment of officers. The railroad is to commence at or near Lexington, pass by or through Richmond and Boone's Gap, and connect to the Lebanon Branch of the Louisville and Nashville railroad.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an act to incorporate a railroad company. It details the corporate structure, capital, and operational route of the company. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

The institution is established to serve a specific racial group, indicating a segregationist purpose.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly names 'Negroes' in its title and purpose, indicating a racially segregated institution. While it is framed as an 'amelioration' of conditions, the explicit racial classification and separation of services aligns with the broader context of Jim Crow laws, which often involved separate but unequal facilities and services for Black individuals. The confidence is high due to the explicit racial designation. The category is 'education' as it pertains to an institution for the deaf, dumb, and blind, which falls under the umbrella of specialized education or care.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the Maysville and Big Sandy Turnpike Road Company
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Maysville and Big Sandy Turnpike Road Company. It details the appointment and duties of a treasurer, the oath required of company officers, the declaration of dividends, and provisions for stockholders to work on road repairs. It also outlines penalties for stockholders who fail to work on the road and authorizes the erection of a toll-gate and the collection of tolls. The statute specifies that stockholders living within one mile of the road are subject to working on its repairs and exempt from working on other roads. It also sets limits on the number of days stockholders can be required to work and establishes penalties for non-compliance, with fines to be used for the road's upkeep. Toll rates are to be consistent with other turnpike roads in the county, with specific provisions for shorter distances and travel between parts of a farm.
keywords: Maysville and Big Sandy Turnpike Road Company

It details the roles of officers, the obligations of stockholders regarding labor and tolls, and the company's financial procedures. classification.title: AN ACT to levy a tax to aid in building Turnpike Roads in Lewis County
classification.summary: This act levies an ad valorem tax of fifty cents per one hundred dollars on land situated on either side of a proposed turnpike road along Salt Lick creek in Lewis County. The tax is intended to fund the construction of this road and is to be collected until fifteen miles of the road from Vanceburg are completed. The collected taxes are to be appropriated towards building the road on Salt Lick creek and its tributaries. The sheriff of Lewis County is responsible for collecting these taxes and remitting them to the road company's treasurer.

It details the tax rate, the lands subject to taxation, and the collection and appropriation of these funds. classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of the City Council of Vanceburg.
classification.summary: This act validates a lease agreement between the City Council of Vanceburg and The Vanceburg Grade Company for the public landing. It authorizes the company to charge fees for the use of the landing, wharf, and grade, with rates not to exceed those in Maysville, Kentucky. The company is responsible for repairs and must post a list of charges. The act also amends a previous act regarding Vanceburg's town governance, establishing the election of a mayor by qualified voters on the first Monday in August annually.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of Common School District No. 23, in McCracken County
classification.summary: This act authorizes the trustees of Common School District No. 23 in McCracken County to levy a tax for common school purposes. Crucially, it explicitly states that the colored population of the district shall not be included in the tax and that colored children shall not be admitted to the common school of the district. The act requires a vote of qualified voters for its adoption.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'colored population' and 'colored children' in the context of taxation and admission to a common school. This directly indicates a racial distinction in the provision of educational services, which is a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. The law mandates separate treatment based on race in education. Therefore, it is classified as a Jim Crow law with an explicit racial indicator. The confidence is high due to the direct mention of race and segregation in education. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'ACT' passed by the General Assembly. The category is 'education' because the statute pertains to common schools.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of Alfred Kelly and others.
classification.summary: This act directs John N. Fitzpatrick, as escheator for Allen county, to settle his accounts regarding the estate of Will Oliver, a deceased freeman of color. The act mandates that Fitzpatrick pay any remaining funds from Oliver's estate, with interest, to Oliver's children (Rebecca, Alfred, Sarah Ann, Eliza, and Matilda), who were slaves at the time of their father's death and thus could not inherit. The property was reportedly accumulated by Oliver after he became a freeman, and the act aims to ensure his now-free children benefit from it.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'freeman of color' and 'slaves' in relation to inheritance rights. While it aims to rectify a historical injustice by allowing the children of a deceased freeman of color to inherit property they were previously barred from due to their enslaved status, it does not enact a discriminatory policy. Instead, it addresses a specific situation where racial status (being enslaved) prevented rightful inheritance. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law, but it does contain explicit racial indicators. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the chapter numbering and the preamble typical of legislative acts.

classification.summary: This act grants W. J. and John Brewer an additional two years to redeem lands sold by the sheriff of Franklin County in December 1866. classification.title: AN ACT to provide for election by the people of all officers of the City of Newport.
classification.summary: This act mandates that all officers of the City of Newport, whether for the entire city or for any ward, shall be elected by qualified voters. It also specifies that city and ward offices currently filled by appointment by the city council shall be filled by election. Vacancies in the board of trustees and visitors of common schools occurring since the last annual city election shall be filled by election for the unexpired terms. However, vacancies in any city office, except for members of the council and school board, may be filled by appointment by the city council until the next annual city election. Vacancies on the school board may be filled by the board itself until the annual election.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the election and appointment of city officers in Newport, Kentucky. It details the process for filling city and ward offices, including provisions for vacancies. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the Charter of the City of Covington.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the City of Covington. It repeals a previous restriction on the salary of the superintendent of common schools, allowing the board of school trustees to set a reasonable compensation. It also grants the board the power to fill vacancies by a two-thirds vote, allows the superintendent to suspend pupils with the right to appeal to the board, mandates that designated textbooks remain unchanged for two years unless by unanimous vote, and repeals a provision requiring viva voce voting at city elections, implying a shift to secret ballots. Finally, it grants the city council power to require houses and tenants to meet certain standards.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an amendment to the charter of the City of Covington. It deals with the administration of common schools, including the compensation of the superintendent, the appointment of school board members, pupil suspension, and textbook selection. It also touches on city council powers regarding housing. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to incorporate the Louisville and Frankfort Railroad Company," approved March 1st, 1847, and to amend the charter of said company.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for land acquisition by the Louisville and Frankfort Railroad Company. It details how engineers and commissioners should value land, assess advantages and disadvantages to adjoining properties, and report these findings to the county clerk. The clerk is then to issue summons to landowners. The presiding judge of the county court has jurisdiction to order the grant of right-of-way upon payment or tender of assessed damages, after deducting advantages. Provisions are made for landowners out of county, non-residents, infants, and lunatics, including the appointment of attorneys and guardians ad litem. The statute also states that the actual value of land taken shall not be diminished by estimated advantages. Commissioners must swear to discharge their duties faithfully.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the legal process of eminent domain for a railroad company. It outlines procedures for land valuation, compensation, and legal notices to landowners. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The procedures described are standard legal processes for land acquisition and do not appear to be related to Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence score. The category is null as it does not fall under any of the specified Jim Crow categories. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the source 'GenAssemb V2-1867.pdf' and the year '1867'.

It outlines the procedures for voter approval of boundary extensions and mandates the division of the city into twelve wards by the general council, ensuring an equal number of qualified voters per ward. It also specifies how the city will pay its portion of taxes to Jefferson County.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The focus is on municipal governance and voter participation in boundary decisions. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act details the process for extending the city boundaries of Louisville, Kentucky. It outlines how the city will handle existing county debts, the apportionment of public expenses between the city and county, and the conditions under which newly annexed land can be taxed. It also addresses the rights of turnpike, plank road, and railway companies within the extended boundaries and prohibits the reopening of unused graveyards for interment.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administrative and financial adjustments required when a city expands its geographical boundaries. It details the division of responsibilities and financial obligations between the city and the county, and the conditions for taxing newly incorporated land. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Louisville
classification.summary: This section of the act grants the general council of Louisville the power to establish and maintain a 'house of reform for white females'. It also allows judges to commit white females to this institution instead of the city work-house or jail for offenses not amounting to a felony. The commitment is limited to one year. The statute explicitly mentions 'white females', indicating a racial distinction in the application of this reformatory institution.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white females' in relation to the establishment and use of a 'house of reform'. This indicates a racial classification for individuals who can be committed to this institution. While it doesn't explicitly mention other races, the explicit mention of 'white' implies a segregation or differential treatment based on race, which is characteristic of Jim Crow-era legislation. The category is 'incarceration' because it deals with a reformatory institution and commitments by judges. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1867.

classification.title: An Act to Constitute the "Commissioners of the Sinking Fund of the City of Louisville"
classification.summary: This act establishes the "Commissioners of the Sinking Fund of the City of Louisville" as a corporate body with powers to manage the city's sinking fund. It outlines the election of a president, filling of vacancies, use of a seal, and the creation of rules and by-laws. The terms of office for commissioners are defined, with specific provisions for the initial election. Eligibility for the office is restricted to those who are not current city officers and possess the qualifications of a councilman. The city treasurer serves as the treasurer and secretary of the board, responsible for the fund's safekeeping and record-keeping. Orders upon the fund require a majority vote of the board, proper entry in records, and certification. The fund can be used to pay debts or interest charges, including those payable in other states or Europe.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the establishment and operational procedures of a sinking fund commission for the City of Louisville. It focuses on the administrative and financial aspects of managing public funds, including the qualifications of commissioners, their terms of office, and the process for making financial disbursements. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly create racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to amend the charter of the city of Louisville," approved March 10, 1865, and to amend the charter of said city.
classification.summary: This act contains several provisions related to the city of Louisville. Section 11 prohibits the placement of indecent or gross advertisements in public view or on private property. Section 12 outlines the eligibility requirements for the city attorney, requiring two years of residency in Jefferson County and current residency in the city. Section 13 details the process by which the general council can extinguish the franchise rights of turnpike, plank road, or railway companies within the city, involving a petition to the Louisville chancery court, a trial (potentially by jury), and payment of compensation. Section 14 states the act takes effect upon passage.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text from 1867 deals with municipal regulations in Louisville, Kentucky. It covers topics such as public advertising, eligibility for city attorney, and the process for discontinuing the rights of transportation companies. None of the sections mention race or contain any provisions that would be considered discriminatory based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Wyoming and Bald Eagle Turnpike Road Company Incorporation
classification.summary: This statute incorporates the Wyoming and Bald Eagle Turnpike Road Company, outlining the process for subscribing to capital stock, electing officers (president and directors), and establishing the company as a body politic and corporate. It grants the company powers to hold property, contract, sue and be sued, and to fix and regulate the elevation, grade, width, and toll rates for the road. The company is authorized to erect toll gates and collect tolls after completing three miles of the road, with proceeds to be used for further completion. It also grants the company the power to enter upon lands and public roads for surveying and examination.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Maysville and Big Sandy Turnpike Company
classification.summary: This statute incorporates the Maysville and Big Sandy Turnpike Company, granting it powers to construct and maintain a turnpike road. It details the company's corporate powers, including capital stock, property acquisition, contracting, suing and being sued, and having a common seal. It also outlines the authority of the president and directors to fix road elevation, grades, width, and toll rates, and to erect toll-gates once a portion of the road is completed. Furthermore, it grants the company the right to enter upon lands for surveying and material acquisition, and establishes a process for obtaining right-of-way through eminent domain if agreement with landowners cannot be reached.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of certain Common School Districts in this State.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Superintendent of Public Instruction to certify and direct payment for common school districts listed, provided certain conditions are met. These conditions include payment only from surplus funds for the school year 1866, at a rate not exceeding previous payments, and only if payment has not already been made. Schools taught for less than three months would be paid only for the time taught. If a county's surplus is insufficient, districts would receive a pro rata share. Reports from these districts must have been received by May 20, 1867.

It mandates an annual election for five trustees who will have the power to require property owners to improve streets and sidewalks at their own expense. of pay ,

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to incorporate the Louisville Agricultural and Mechanical Association," approved March 11, 1867, and to authorize the trustees of the town of Lancaster to subscribe for stock in said association.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act incorporating the Louisville Agricultural and Mechanical Association. It specifically grants the trustees of the town of Lancaster the authority to subscribe for stock in this association, up to a value of five thousand dollars. To fund this subscription, the trustees are empowered to issue town bonds, also not exceeding five thousand dollars, with a maturity of five years and an interest rate of eight percent. The act also allows for the early repayment of these bonds and details how they should be issued and validated. Furthermore, it authorizes the trustees to levy a tax of ten cents on each one hundred dollars of taxable property within Lancaster to cover the principal and interest of these bonds.
classification.reasoning: The statute text provided details an act authorizing the town of Lancaster to invest in the Louisville Agricultural and Mechanical Association and to issue bonds and levy taxes to support this investment. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in 1867. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about municipal finance and corporate investment, with no racial implications.

classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Winchester and Mount Sterling Turnpike Road Company. It outlines conditions for the consolidation of roads, including the full payment of indebtedness and a review by appointed commissioners to ensure good repair. It also specifies how Clark County's indebtedness for road construction will be handled and how tolls will be divided and applied after consolidation, with a condition that the Clark County portion of the road must be in good order and of a specified width before consolidation.

classification.title: AN ACT to Protect the Farmers of Boone and Other Counties against Certain Trespasses
classification.summary: This act prohibits trespassing on posted farmlands for hunting or taking property without consent. It establishes penalties for non-residents (immediate arrest, $10 fine, 5 days jail if unpaid) and residents ($10 fine recoverable by execution). It also allows for civil action for damages. Justices of the peace have jurisdiction, and defendants can request a jury trial. Landowners must provide notice by publishing in a local newspaper and posting on the courthouse door for one month.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses trespassing on farmland and establishes penalties for violations. It differentiates penalties based on residency (non-resident vs. resident of Kentucky), but not on race. There are no explicit or implicit racial classifications or restrictions within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in a specific year. Confidence is high because the statute's focus is clearly on property rights and residency, not race.

classification.title: AN ACT to charter the Louisville Young Men's Christian Association.
classification.summary: This act charters the Louisville Young Men's Christian Association, outlining its corporate powers and membership requirements. Active members must be exclusively male members in good standing of Evangelical churches in the state. The association is granted perpetual succession, the power to adopt a seal, contract, sue and be sued, and acquire property up to $50,000, which shall be free of taxation. The officers are defined as a president, vice presidents from each represented Evangelical denomination, secretaries, a treasurer, registrar, and librarian.

classification.summary: This act incorporates the Flemingsburg and Mayslick Turnpike Road Company, authorizing the formation of a company with a capital not exceeding thirty thousand dollars for the construction of a macadamized road between Flemingsburg and Mayslick. The road is to intersect the Elizaville and Helena road at a convenient point.

The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the content is clearly unrelated to Jim Crow legislation.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the Charter of the City of Covington.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the City of Covington. It allows the city council, upon the mayor's recommendation, to appoint a suitable resident to hold the mayor's court in the mayor's absence or inability to attend. This appointed person will have the same powers and jurisdiction as the mayor and receive proportional fees and salary. It also repeals a previous act requiring a justice of the peace to hold the mayor's court. The act further states that in criminal and penal cases tried in the mayor's court, if the fine exceeds five dollars...

It outlines procedures for filling the role of the mayor in their absence and addresses the repeal of a previous provision. The text does not contain any language, explicit or implicit, that suggests racial discrimination or segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Jamestown
classification.summary: This act details the election of councilmen, president of the council, street commissioner, city clerk, city assessor, and treasurer for the city of Jamestown. It outlines the powers and duties of the president and council, including their authority to borrow money, levy and collect taxes, fix salaries of officers, and license various businesses and exhibitions. The statute also specifies that individuals failing to pay the poll tax will not be qualified to vote in city elections.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the municipal governance and fiscal powers of the city of Jamestown. It outlines election procedures, duties of officials, and the city's authority to tax and license. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The poll tax mentioned is a general requirement for voting eligibility and does not appear to be racially motivated in this context, especially given the absence of any racial qualifiers. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the City of Dayton
classification.summary: This act incorporates the city of Dayton, Kentucky, establishing the positions of mayor and city marshal, outlining their terms of office, powers, and duties. It also details the treasurer's responsibilities for tax collection. The act specifies that existing laws applicable to the town of Jamestown, unless inconsistent, will apply to the city of Dayton.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the jurisdiction of the quarterly court of Kenton county and the jurisdiction of its presiding judge.
classification.summary: This act modifies the jurisdiction of the quarterly court of Kenton county, extending its reach to legal or equitable actions up to $300, exclusive of interest and costs. It also grants the presiding judge of the quarterly court of Kenton county the same jurisdiction as justices of the peace within the corporate limits of Covington for cases up to $50, exclusive of interest and costs. The act further outlines procedures for defendants who believe they cannot receive a fair trial before the judge, allowing for the transfer of their case to a justice of the peace. This provision also applies to non-resident defendants served within Covington.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the jurisdictional limits and procedural rules for the quarterly court and its presiding judge in Kenton county, Kentucky. It outlines monetary thresholds for cases and provides a mechanism for defendants to request a change of venue to a justice of the peace if they believe a fair trial cannot be obtained. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The focus is purely on judicial administration and civil procedure. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate the Bryantsville and Sugar Creek Turnpike Road Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Bryantsville and Sugar Creek Turnpike Road Company, outlining its purpose to construct an artificial road in Garrard County, Kentucky. It specifies the company's name, the route of the road, the capital stock, and the process for opening books for stock subscriptions. It also includes provisions for filling vacancies in the board of trustees and for the election of police officers and town marshals. Additionally, it grants trustees the power to open streets and alleys and repeals a previous act related to selling alleys and cross-streets. Trustees are required to take an oath of office, and all prior acts incorporating the town of Prestonsburg are repealed. The legislature reserves the right to alter, amend, or repeal this act, which takes effect upon its passage.
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains two distinct acts. The first part (sections 564 to § 18) deals with the incorporation and governance of the town of Prestonsburg, including provisions for elections, street openings, and trustee oaths. The second part (CHAPTER 2068) incorporates the Bryantsville and Sugar Creek Turnpike Road Company. Neither of these acts contains any language or provisions that suggest racial discrimination or segregation. They are administrative and corporate in nature, related to local governance and infrastructure development. Therefore, they are classified as 'no' for Jim Crow laws, with a confidence of 1.0, and the category is null. The racial indicator is 'none'.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the River Packet Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the River Packet Company with a capital stock of fifty thousand dollars. The company is granted perpetual succession and the power to sue and be sued, have a common seal, and make by-laws. Its business will involve transporting persons and property to and from Louisville along the Ohio river and its tributaries, and other rivers as needed. The company can own, construct, repair, charter, hire, sell, and convey steamboats and other watercraft, as well as acquire and sell land, landings, and wharves. Commissioners are appointed to receive stock subscriptions, and upon sufficient paid-in stock, a meeting will be held to elect a board of directors. The directors will then elect a president and other necessary officers.
classification.reasoning: The statute clearly outlines the incorporation and operational powers of a river packet company. It details the company's capital, its ability to conduct business, and the process for its governance. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to Prevent the Sale of Spirituous, Malt, or Vinous Liquors in the Town of Grundy, or within one mile of its Limits.
classification.summary: This act prohibits the granting of licenses to sell spirituous, malt, or vinous liquors within the town of Grundy or within one mile of its limits, effective February 12, 1867. Druggists and apothecaries may be licensed to sell for medicinal purposes only, requiring a physician's order or a sworn statement of necessity for family use. The act also defines retailers as those selling less than ten gallons at a time and mandates removal of ten gallons or more at the time of sale.

classification.summary: This act allows plaintiffs identified as 'Sidney and others, of color' to request a change of venue in a pending Madison circuit court case if they file an affidavit stating they cannot receive a fair trial due to undue influence and prejudice against them.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'of color' in the title and refers to plaintiffs as such. While it grants a procedural right (change of venue), the explicit racial designation and the context of potential prejudice against this group strongly suggest it is related to the legal framework that would become characteristic of Jim Crow era laws, where racial distinctions were often made in legal proceedings. The ability to request a change of venue due to prejudice against a racial group is a mechanism that could be used to address or, in some contexts, reinforce racial disparities in the justice system. Therefore, it is classified as a Jim Crow law with an explicit racial indicator. The confidence is high due to the explicit mention of race and the nature of the legal relief sought.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Cincinnati, Covington, and Cumberland Gap Railroad Company
classification.summary: This statute incorporates the Cincinnati, Covington, and Cumberland Gap Railroad Company. It outlines the process for subscribing stock, organizing the company, electing officers, and managing its affairs. The text details the requirements for stock subscriptions, the number of directors, the election of a president, secretary, and treasurer, and the responsibilities of the treasurer regarding company funds and accounts. It also specifies the bond required for the treasurer and the authorization needed for collecting money.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the incorporation and operational procedures for a railroad company. It focuses on financial aspects, governance, and administrative duties. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company
classification.summary: This act details the organizational structure, financial regulations, and operational rights of the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company. It covers aspects like the qualifications of officers, the board's authority to fill vacancies, contract binding, limitations on contracts, exclusive rights of transportation, and the process for calling for stock payments. It also grants the company the right to farm out its transportation services and allows the General Assembly to regulate freight and fare rates.

It focuses on corporate governance, financial management, and transportation rights. classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company
classification.summary: This act details provisions for the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company, including stock forfeiture for non-payment, stock transfer procedures, capital increases, annual reporting requirements, voting rights of stockholders, and the company's power to acquire land and property necessary for its operations. It specifies that any state, citizen, corporation, or company can subscribe to and hold stock, with the same rights and liabilities as other stockholders. The voting scale is one vote per share, with a maximum of one thousand votes per individual, corporation, or company.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company
classification.summary: This statute grants the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company the right to construct its road, including branches, across public roads and watercourses, provided navigation is not permanently obstructed. It also allows the company to purchase bridges and turnpike roads, and outlines a process for acquiring land and rights-of-way through eminent domain, involving valuation by commissioners and potential jury trials. The statute focuses on the operational and land acquisition powers of the railroad company.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the powers and rights of a railroad company regarding construction, land acquisition, and infrastructure. It does not contain any provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race, nor does it mention race at all. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT, entitled "An act to amend the Charter of the Citizens' Bank of Louisville."
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Citizens' Bank of Louisville. It authorizes the bank to receive its own stock in payment of debts owed to it and to increase its capital stock up to five hundred thousand dollars. The act also specifies that the president must pay fifty cents per hundred dollars of stock into the treasury annually, which is part of the Sinking Fund and in lieu of all other taxes or bonuses to the state. It also outlines penalties for bank officers who embezzle funds or falsify records, classifying it as a felony punishable by ten to twenty years in the penitentiary. Additionally, it prohibits the bank from issuing notes to be used as money and limits interest rates to six percent per annum. The General Assembly reserves the right to examine the bank's affairs.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details amendments to the charter of the Citizens' Bank of Louisville. It covers financial regulations, penalties for fraud, and operational authorizations for the bank. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in 1867. The confidence is high because the content is clearly financial and corporate in nature, with no discernible racial component.

classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'of color', indicating a racial component. However, the act itself is procedural, authorizing a change of venue, and does not appear to establish discriminatory practices or separate facilities. It is a legal process that happens to involve individuals of color. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law, but the racial indicator is explicit due to the wording.

classification.title: Acts for the Benefit of Various Individuals and Entities
classification.summary: This index lists various legislative acts passed for the benefit of specific individuals, including sheriffs, late officials, and sureties, across different Kentucky counties. It also includes an act to incorporate a benevolent society and an act authorizing a change of venue in a case involving individuals 'of color'.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is an index of legislative acts from 1867. Most entries are for acts 'for the benefit of' specific individuals, often former officials, across various counties. These are administrative or financial matters and do not inherently suggest Jim Crow legislation. However, one entry, 'Sidney and others, of color, act authorizing change of venue in the case of, against May's administrator', explicitly mentions 'of color'. While this indicates a racial distinction in a legal proceeding, the nature of the act (change of venue) does not, on its own, classify it as a Jim Crow law. Jim Crow laws typically involve segregation, disenfranchisement, or other discriminatory practices. A change of venue is a procedural matter that could be requested for various reasons, not exclusively discriminatory ones. Therefore, while race is mentioned, the act itself is not definitively a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high that it's not a Jim Crow law because the majority of the entries are clearly not, and the one with a racial indicator is procedural and not inherently discriminatory in the context of Jim Crow. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it's an index of acts passed by the General Assembly in a specific year.
